config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent ID vs US baseline block. Use this before exploratory variants.
config.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. I do not think society should automatically take priority over the rights of the individual, because once individual rights are treated as secondary, it becomes too easy for governments or majorities to justify abuse. In my view, society is strongest when it protects basic individual freedoms while still asking people to accept reasonable limits for the common good. From an Indonesian perspective, I also value community, but I have seen how easily "the public interest" can be used to silence dissent or ignore personal dignity.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The rights of the individual should not be sacrificed just because society claims a greater good. From an American perspective, I am wary of giving society blanket priority, because history shows how easily majorities and governments can trample minorities when “the common good” becomes a blank check. At the same time, I do not believe individual rights are absolute in every case, since things like public safety, disease control, and basic law require real limits. The better principle is that society can place narrow, justified restrictions, but only if it can prove they are necessary and do not erase core rights.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think the interests of society should automatically take priority over the rights of the individual. In Indonesia, we value gotong royong and social harmony, but I have also seen how appeals to “the public good” can be used to pressure people into silence or excuse unfair treatment. The most recent point about narrow, justified limits is important, but those limits must be truly exceptional and carefully checked, otherwise society becomes an excuse for majority power. A healthy society protects shared interests by protecting individual dignity first, not by treating individual rights as disposable.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take priority over the rights of the individual as a general rule. From a United States perspective, I am especially suspicious of any claim that the common good automatically outranks personal liberty, because that kind of logic has too often been used to justify censorship, discrimination, and abuse by majorities or governments. That said, I do not see rights as unlimited in practice: public health, safety, and basic legal order sometimes require real constraints, but those constraints should be narrow, evidence-based, and open to challenge. A society is healthiest when it treats individual rights as the default and only limits them when it can clearly prove necessity.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take priority over the rights of the individual as a general rule. From an Indonesian perspective, I value gotong royong and social harmony, but I do not trust “society” as a blanket excuse to override personal dignity, because that can easily become pressure from the majority or from authorities. The point about narrow, evidence-based limits is reasonable, but in practice those limits must be tightly controlled, or they become a convenient way to silence dissent and punish the vulnerable. A good society protects the common good by protecting rights first, not by treating individuals as expendable.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take priority over the rights of the individual as a general rule. From an American perspective, I do not trust “the common good” when it is used as a blank check, because that has too often been the language behind censorship, discrimination, and government overreach. The point about narrow, evidence-based limits is important, but those limits have to stay truly limited and challengeable, or they stop being protections and become tools of control. In my view, a society is strongest when individual rights are the default and any restriction must be clearly justified.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
